demos.evidence_text: He reports increased reliability in tracking events using a seizure diary corroborated by his group facilitator. This month he experienced two clusters this month; each ~five focal impaired-awareness seizures, typically occurring over 24–36 hours
demos.note_text:
Epilepsy Centre 

Neurosciences Division 

Clinic Date: 02 October 2025 

Dr Patel  
St George's University Hospitals NHS Foundation Trust  
Neurology Outpatient Department, Blackshaw Road, London SW17 0QT  

Dear Dr Patel,  

James Walker, DOB: 14-08-1987, Hospital No: H928374 NHS No. 7392015468  
12 Brookside Close, Wimbledon, London SW19 3DA  

Diagnoses: 

Recurrent unprovoked seizures; aetiology and classification under ongoing evaluation. 

I reviewed James Walker in clinic today with his partner present. He remains engaged with a local peer support group, and observations from peers have supplemented his history between appointments. He reports increased reliability in tracking events using a seizure diary corroborated by his group facilitator. This month he experienced two clusters this month; each ~five focal impaired-awareness seizures, typically occurring over 24–36 hours, with brief post-ictal confusion and fatigue but no injuries. There have been no witnessed generalised tonic–clonic events, no myoclonic jerks, and no nocturnal tongue biting reported. 

Precipitants noted by the patient and peers include sleep deprivation and high work-related stress. Alcohol intake is minimal and unchanged. He denies missed medication doses. There is no new neurological symptomatology, and no intercurrent illness. 

Medication: Levetiracetam 1 g twice daily continued without adverse effects beyond mild irritability, which he manages with scheduled exercise and mindfulness sessions recommended by the peer group. He carries buccal midazolam but has not required its use this year. 

Investigations: Previous MRI brain (2023) non-diagnostic. Interictal EEG (April 2024) showed non-specific changes without definitive lateralising or localising features. We have arranged an ambulatory EEG with event marker to capture a cluster if possible. Routine bloods today (U&E, LFT, FBC) were requested to monitor general health while on therapy. 

Plan: 
- Continue levetiracetam 1 g twice daily. 
- Proceed with ambulatory EEG; patient instructed to trigger the event marker at aura onset and obtain peer or partner corroboration where feasible. 
- Maintain seizure diary; continue engagement with the peer support group to enhance collateral reporting. 
- Safety advice re-emphasised: supervised bathing, cooking precautions, and avoidance of heights during periods of sleep disruption. Driving status remains suspended in accordance with DVLA guidance; we reviewed the requirements today. 
- Review in clinic in three months, or sooner if cluster frequency increases or if any generalised convulsive event occurs. 

Yours sincerely
demos.reasoning: The note states the patient experienced two clusters this month, each cluster consisting of approximately five focal impaired-awareness seizures. The clusters occurred over 24–36 hours. The highest quantified seizure frequency is the cluster frequency, which is 2 clusters per month, with 5 seizures per cluster.

demos.evidence_text: three nocturnal clusters this month; each ~5 or 6 events
demos.note_text:
St Mary's Hospital 

Institute of Neurology 

Clinic Date: 02 October 2025 

Dr Patel  
St Mary's Hospital  
Institute of Neurology, St Mary's Hospital, London W2 1NY  

Dear Dr Patel,  

Sophie Williams, DOB: 24-11-1991, Hospital No: N483720 NHS No. 9054412276  
12 Fernbank Close, London, W10 6QL 

Medication: 

Levetiracetam 1 g twice daily. Lamotrigine 100 mg morning / 150 mg night. Midazolam 5 mg buccal PRN for prolonged clusters (not used this month). 

I reviewed Sophie Williams in neurology clinic with collateral information from employer occupational health, who provide routine summaries aligned to her wellness checks. She has Combined Generalised and Focal Epilepsy Type, with a history of both generalised tonic–clonic seizures and focal aware/aware-to-impaired awareness events. She reports three nocturnal clusters this month; each ~5 or 6 events, witnessed by her partner and corroborated by occupational health logs noting next‑day fatigue and headache. These predominantly occur between 02:00–04:00, start with a brief epigastric rising sensation and left hand tingling (suggestive of focal onset), and on two occasions progressed to a brief generalised convulsion lasting under a minute without injury. Daytime events have not been reported in the last eight weeks. 

She denies missed doses and uses a pill dispenser with phone reminders. Occupational health notes show good adherence and no concerns at work performance aside from transient post‑ictal tiredness on mornings after nocturnal activity; she has not required rescue medication. Triggers discussed include poor sleep continuity during a recent shift change and increased caffeine intake; she has already reduced caffeine and is keeping a sleep log. There is no intercurrent illness, no alcohol misuse, and pregnancy is not planned. 

We discussed that her epilepsy includes both generalised and focal elements, and that nocturnal clustering may reflect disturbed sleep architecture. She will continue current doses while we gather additional data. I have requested: 

- 72‑hour ambulatory EEG with sleep capture to characterise onset and generalisation. 
- Serum levetiracetam and lamotrigine levels for reference. 
- Updated MRI epilepsy protocol (last imaging 2019). 
- Employer occupational health to continue monthly summaries as a collateral source. 

Safety: She does not drive. Seizure first aid and SUDEP discussions were revisited; her partner has the rescue plan and knows when to call emergency services. She has a written seizure plan shared with occupational health. 

Plan: 
- No change to regular medication today. 
- Continue seizure diary noting time, semiology, and any injuries; attach occupational health monthly summaries. 
- If clusters increase in frequency, duration, or occur with injury, or if single seizures exceed 5 minutes, she will activate her rescue plan. 
- Review in three months, sooner if deterioration. Results will guide whether to adjust lamotrigine (night dose) or consider adjunctive therapy. 

Yours sincerely
demos.reasoning: The note states that Sophie Williams reports "three nocturnal clusters this month; each ~5 or 6 events." This indicates 3 clusters per month, with approximately 5 to 6 seizures per cluster. The note also specifies no daytime events in the last eight weeks, so the highest frequency is the cluster frequency. The label should reflect the cluster frequency and the number of seizures per cluster.
demos.seizure_frequency_number: 3 cluster per month, 5 to 6 per cluster

demos.evidence_text: He reports that he has been Seizure-free since 13-Nov-2015
demos.note_text:
Department of Neurology

Clinic Date: 18 July 2016

Dr A. Patel
St Cuthbert’s Neurology Clinic
Level 3, Outpatients, St Cuthbert’s Hospital, York YO1 7AB

Dear Dr A. Patel,

Michael Turner, DOB: 22-03-1987, Hospital No: H392774 NHS No. 9437710256
Flat 12, 5 Mill Lane, York, YO10 3BG

Diagnoses:

Recurrent unprovoked seizures; aetiology not established. Prior EEG in community reportedly non-diagnostic.

Current Medication:

Lamotrigine 75 mg twice daily

Plan:

Continue current antiepileptic medication. Reinforce sleep hygiene and trigger management. Safety counselling and personalised plan held on record.

I reviewed Michael Turner in clinic today. He reports that he has been Seizure-free since 13-Nov-2015. Prior to that date he described occasional cluster patterns associated with sleep loss, and he has since focused on regular sleep routines and avoiding sleep deprivation. He also confirms that a personalised self-management and safety plan is documented elsewhere in his notes and remains up to date.

On examination today there are no focal neurological deficits. He is tolerating Lamotrigine 75 mg twice daily without adverse effects. Given the current stability and absence of events since November 2015, I have advised continuing the present regimen and maintaining vigilance around recognised triggers, particularly sleep loss. He has a documented plan for what to do should clusters recur, and he understands the escalation steps.

I have not arranged a routine follow-up; however, I am happy to review him sooner if there are any breakthrough events or medication concerns. Otherwise, we can consider review in due course to reassess treatment duration in the context of ongoing stability.

With best wishes,
demos.reasoning: The note states the patient has been seizure-free since 13-Nov-2015, and the clinic date is 18 July 2016, which is more than 6 months of seizure freedom. Therefore, the appropriate label is "seizure free for N unit" with N being the number of months since the last seizure. From November 13, 2015 to July 18, 2016 is approximately 8 months.

demos.note_text:
King's College Hospital 

Department of Neurosciences 

Clinic Date: 02 October 2025 

Dr A. Patel
Consultant Neurologist  
King's College Hospital  
Department of Neurosciences, King's College Hospital, London SE5 9RS  

Dear Dr A. Patel
Consultant Neurologist,  

Daniel Carter, DOB: 14-03-1986, Hospital No: KCH728311 NHS No. 9468201573  
12 Rowan Close, Bromley, BR2 8LP

Thank you for asking me to review your patient. He works rotating shifts, including nights, and reports long-standing sleep disturbance with variable bedtimes and fragmented rest. Despite the circadian rhythm disruption, he has been Free of seizures for 1 year by his account and corroborated by his partner and workplace records (no incidents reported on occupational health logs). There have been no witnessed collapses, no nocturnal events, and no injuries or tongue biting in that period. He denies any recent auras, automatisms, or post-event confusion. He carries a personal seizure action plan but has not needed to use it over the last year. ï¿½

He does describe episodic fatigue on switching from nights to early shifts, transient difficulty with concentration, and occasional headaches at the end of a run of nights. These appear temporally linked to sleep restriction rather than to paroxysmal events. There is no new focal neurological symptomatology. He remains on a stable dose of his regular anti-seizure medication and is fully adherent; there have been no recent dose changes, drug interactions, or missed doses. Occupationally he has continued in his role without incident, and managerial reports confirm punctual attendance and no safety alerts. Ã©

On examination today he was alert and cooperative. Speech, language, and cognition were grossly intact on bedside testing. Cranial nerves were normal. Motor and sensory examinations were unremarkable. Coordination and gait were normal. Cardiovascular observations were stable. There were no stigmata of medication toxicity. 

Investigations: Routine bloods earlier this month (FBC, U&E, LFTs, bone profile, vitamin D) were within reference ranges. ECG today shows normal sinus rhythm with no conduction delay. Prior MRI brain and EEG (from last year) did not yield a unifying diagnosis. I note that clinical classification remains under review; at present I cannot assign a definitive seizure syndrome. â€

Assessment: In the context of ongoing rotating shift work with disrupted circadian rhythm, he remains clinically stable and Free of seizures for 1 year. There is no current evidence of breakthrough events. The principal risks at present relate to sleep debt and fatigue rather than active paroxysmal activity. 

Plan:
- Continue current anti-seizure medication at the same dose; no changes today. 
- Sleep and shift hygiene: patient has implemented scheduled dark-phase sleep after night shifts, gradual phase shifts where possible, and limited caffeine after midday; he will continue these measures. 
- Occupational health: he will share this letter with his employer. Current duties can continue as is; no specific restrictions requested from me at this time. 
- Safety: standard precautions maintained; he carries an employer-notified care plan though unused for 12 months. 
- Follow-up: review in 6–9 months, or sooner if there is any recurrence, injury, loss of awareness, or concerning nocturnal behaviour reported. 

Please let me know if there is additional information from primary care or occupational health that would help longitudinal monitoring. 

Yours sincerely
demos.reasoning: The note clearly states the patient has been "Free of seizures for 1 year" with corroboration from partner and workplace records, and no incidents reported. This indicates seizure freedom for at least one year, which meets the criteria for labeling as "seizure free for 1 year."

fields.description: Seizure frequency in Gan label format. One of: '{count} per {unit}' e.g. '2 per week', '1 per 3 month'; '{count} cluster per {unit}, {n} per cluster'; 'unknown'; 'no seizure frequency reference'; or null to abstain. Unit must be singular: day/week/month/year.
Shortest exact contiguous quote from the note supporting the label. Do not include prompt instructions or wrapper text. Null if abstaining.

signature.instructions:
Extract the Gan seizure-frequency label and supporting evidence from a clinical note.

/no_think
Do not use hidden reasoning. Emit only the requested output fields.

The label must match the Gan annotation vocabulary exactly. Abstain (null) only when
the note contains no usable seizure-frequency information.

Synthesis-backed policy:
- Output only canonical Gan labels: N per unit, N to M per unit, N per M unit,
  N cluster per unit, M per cluster, seizure free for N unit, unknown, or
  no seizure frequency reference.
- Use singular units: day, week, month, year.
- Convert daily/nightly to 1 per day, every N unit to 1 per N unit, and
  numeric "or" ranges to "to" ranges.
- Choose the highest current quantified seizure-type frequency.
- Use the full cluster format; do not drop either the cluster period or
  the per-cluster count.
- Use seizure free for N unit only when seizure freedom is at least 6 months.
  If seizure freedom is shorter than 6 months, compute the rate from the
  total events over the described period.
- For year-to-date counts, use months elapsed since January as the denominator.
- Treat "over the last quarter" as an observation window when the note gives
  a per-month rate; do not automatically convert it to per 3 month.
- evidence_text must be an exact contiguous quote from the note.
